CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): Overall the paper is well organized (intro → method → experiments → conclusion) and the main contributions—the multigraph formulation and its claimed O(nT) inference—are stated early. Notation appears consistent and definitions are provided for the log-linear model and generalized inside–outside algorithm, and figures/tables/examples effectively illustrate empirical comparisons on ACE2004/2005 and GENIA. Writing is generally good, though a few sections would benefit from clearer signposting when asserting theoretical complexity and from explicitly highlighting limitations of the modeling assumptions. As a presentation-focused reviewer I find only minor issues preventing an excellent (5) rating. | B(4): The paper is well-organized with a clear logical flow from introduction to method and experiments, and its main contributions (a multigraph-based structured prediction model with O(nT) inference) are stated early. Notation appears consistent and the methodology (log-linear model, generalized inside–outside, MPE decoding) is clearly described; experiments report standard metrics and hyperparameters and compare to appropriate baselines on ACE2004/2005 and GENIA. Figures, tables, and examples are reported as effective and the writing quality is good, though a few sections could benefit from slightly more concrete implementation details (e.g., code release) to improve reproducibility and clarity for practitioners.

reviewer_a.justification: Overall the paper is well organized (intro → method → experiments → conclusion) and the main contributions—the multigraph formulation and its claimed O(nT) inference—are stated early. Notation appears consistent and definitions are provided for the log-linear model and generalized inside–outside algorithm, and figures/tables/examples effectively illustrate empirical comparisons on ACE2004/2005 and GENIA. Writing is generally good, though a few sections would benefit from clearer signposting when asserting theoretical complexity and from explicitly highlighting limitations of the modeling assumptions. As a presentation-focused reviewer I find only minor issues preventing an excellent (5) rating.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper is well-organized with a clear logical flow from introduction to method and experiments, and its main contributions (a multigraph-based structured prediction model with O(nT) inference) are stated early. Notation appears consistent and the methodology (log-linear model, generalized inside–outside, MPE decoding) is clearly described; experiments report standard metrics and hyperparameters and compare to appropriate baselines on ACE2004/2005 and GENIA. Figures, tables, and examples are reported as effective and the writing quality is good, though a few sections could benefit from slightly more concrete implementation details (e.g., code release) to improve reproducibility and clarity for practitioners.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper compares against a strong set of relevant baselines (mention hypergraph, linear-chain CRF, semi-CRF, constituency parser, Illinois NER) on standard datasets (ACE2004/2005, GENIA) using the same metrics (P/R/F1), and reports hyperparameter settings — which supports largely fair comparisons. As a theoretical purist, I note minor omissions: the summary does not state whether all training/inference conditions (e.g., regularization, feature sets, optimization convergence criteria) were strictly matched to baselines, nor whether any recent alternative overlapping-entity models beyond the listed ones were included. The authors do acknowledge limitations where prior methods perform comparably for some interactions. Overall the comparisons are good with only minor gaps in experimental parity and breadth of competing approaches. | B(4): The paper compares to a strong set of relevant baselines (mention hypergraph, linear-chain CRF, semi-CRF, constituency parser, Illinois NER) and evaluates on standard datasets (ACE2004/2005, GENIA) using Precision/Recall/F1, which supports fair head-to-head comparison. It also reports matching inference complexity (O(nT)) and states a concrete limitation about interactions not modeled, indicating reasonable transparency. While related work coverage is described as adequate and hyperparameters are reported, the summary does not indicate code release or explicit discussion of cases where prior methods match or beat the proposal, so comparisons appear good but not exhaustively conclusive.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper compares against a strong set of relevant baselines (mention hypergraph, linear-chain CRF, semi-CRF, constituency parser, Illinois NER) on standard datasets (ACE2004/2005, GENIA) using the same metrics (P/R/F1), and reports hyperparameter settings — which supports largely fair comparisons. As a theoretical purist, I note minor omissions: the summary does not state whether all training/inference conditions (e.g., regularization, feature sets, optimization convergence criteria) were strictly matched to baselines, nor whether any recent alternative overlapping-entity models beyond the listed ones were included. The authors do acknowledge limitations where prior methods perform comparably for some interactions. Overall the comparisons are good with only minor gaps in experimental parity and breadth of competing approaches.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper compares to a strong set of relevant baselines (mention hypergraph, linear-chain CRF, semi-CRF, constituency parser, Illinois NER) and evaluates on standard datasets (ACE2004/2005, GENIA) using Precision/Recall/F1, which supports fair head-to-head comparison. It also reports matching inference complexity (O(nT)) and states a concrete limitation about interactions not modeled, indicating reasonable transparency. While related work coverage is described as adequate and hyperparameters are reported, the summary does not indicate code release or explicit discussion of cases where prior methods match or beat the proposal, so comparisons appear good but not exhaustively conclusive.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper does introduce a clearly new modeling perspective—using multigraphs for structured prediction of overlapping entities—which appears to be a notable departure from prior hypergraph and constituency-based approaches. However, from the provided summary the technical advance reads as a non-trivial but incremental extension of existing structured representations (mention hypergraphs) rather than a fundamentally new algorithmic paradigm; the authors claim O(nT) inference analogous to prior linear-time models but no summary-level evidence of formal proofs or theoretical guarantees is given, which tempers the novelty claim. Overall the contribution would likely inform experts by offering a new representational viewpoint, but its originality is reduced by similarity to past work and the lack of presented formal complexity/expressivity analysis. | B(4): The paper proposes a clearly new modeling perspective — applying multigraph-based structured prediction to overlapping entity recognition — and explicitly positions this as the first structured prediction model using multigraphs. It differentiates itself from prior work (e.g., mention hypergraph, constituency-based and semi-CRF approaches) and retains linear-time O(nT) inference, which is a non-trivial and practically useful extension. From an applied-NLP viewpoint the idea is notable and informative, though it builds on established components (log-linear training, inside–outside style DP) so the contribution feels significant but not a radical departure from existing sequence/graph methods.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper does introduce a clearly new modeling perspective—using multigraphs for structured prediction of overlapping entities—which appears to be a notable departure from prior hypergraph and constituency-based approaches. However, from the provided summary the technical advance reads as a non-trivial but incremental extension of existing structured representations (mention hypergraphs) rather than a fundamentally new algorithmic paradigm; the authors claim O(nT) inference analogous to prior linear-time models but no summary-level evidence of formal proofs or theoretical guarantees is given, which tempers the novelty claim. Overall the contribution would likely inform experts by offering a new representational viewpoint, but its originality is reduced by similarity to past work and the lack of presented formal complexity/expressivity analysis.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper proposes a clearly new modeling perspective — applying multigraph-based structured prediction to overlapping entity recognition — and explicitly positions this as the first structured prediction model using multigraphs. It differentiates itself from prior work (e.g., mention hypergraph, constituency-based and semi-CRF approaches) and retains linear-time O(nT) inference, which is a non-trivial and practically useful extension. From an applied-NLP viewpoint the idea is notable and informative, though it builds on established components (log-linear training, inside–outside style DP) so the contribution feels significant but not a radical departure from existing sequence/graph methods.

IMPACT.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper provides a useful, practical advance for overlapping entity recognition that preserves linear-time inference and improves empirical performance on standard benchmarks, but has limitations (missing formal proofs, incomplete modeling of some interactions, and unclear code availability). Given its likely adoption by practitioners and potential to be extended, yet noting it is not fully transformative without stronger theoretical guarantees and reproducibility, a score of 4 reflects strong but not groundbreaking impact.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper tackles the important problem of overlapping entity recognition and introduces a novel multigraph-based structured prediction model that reportedly matches the linear-time O(nT) inference of the mention hypergraph while improving empirical F1 on ACE and GENIA datasets. From a theoretical-structured-prediction perspective the contribution is interesting because it extends the repertoire of structured models to multigraph formulations, but its long-term influence is limited by the absence of formal proofs about complexity/expressivity, lack of discussion on exactness vs. approximation of inference, and the admitted limitation in modeling interactions between entity types. Thus the work is a useful, practically-relevant advance likely to be built upon, but not yet transformative without stronger theoretical guarantees.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper tackles the practical and widely-recognized problem of overlapping entity recognition and proposes a principled, structured multigraph model that reportedly matches linear-time inference (O(nT)) while improving empirical performance on standard benchmarks (ACE2004/2005, GENIA) relative to strong baselines. From my applied-NLP perspective this is a useful, implementable advance that practitioners and researchers are likely to adopt or extend, especially since it preserves favorable runtime properties. Impact is tempered because some interaction types remain unmodeled (noted limitation) and code availability is not indicated, which reduces immediate reproducibility and uptake, so I judge the likely influence as strong but not transformative.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper is a strong fit for top NLP/ML venues, addressing an important problem with a novel multigraph structured model and solid empirical evaluation on standard datasets against strong baselines. While Reviewer A raised a reasonable request for deeper theoretical characterization and formal proofs of claimed complexity, this is a minor concern relative to the clear empirical and methodological contributions, so I award the highest appropriateness score.

reviewer_a.justification: This paper addresses a core NLP/structured-prediction problem (overlapping NER) with a novel multigraph-based structured model, compares against appropriate baselines (mention hypergraph, semi-CRF, constituency-based parsing) on standard datasets (ACE2004/2005, GENIA), and uses expected metrics (P/R/F1), so it is broadly within the scope and audience of a top NLP/ML venue. As a theoretical purist I note minor scope concerns: the authors claim O(nT) inference and introduce a new structured representation but the summary lacks formal proofs/complexity theorems and detailed optimization/learning analysis (e.g., likelihood surface, convergence of L-BFGS), which top-tier reviewers in this community typically expect alongside empirical gains. Overall the work is a good fit but would benefit from stronger theoretical characterization to be a perfect match.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper addresses an important practical problem (overlapping NER) well within the scope of top NLP/ML venues, proposing a novel multigraph structured-prediction model and demonstrating empirical gains on standard datasets (ACE2004/2005, GENIA) against strong baselines (mention hypergraph, semi-CRF, constituency parser, Illinois NER). The methodology and evaluation (regularized log-likelihood training, O(nT) inference, precision/recall/F1 reporting, hyperparameters provided) meet community standards and the claimed linear-time complexity plus practical improvements align with my emphasis on usable, reproducible advances. My only minor concern is the absence of an explicit code release in the summary, which would further strengthen reproducibility, but this does not detract from venue fit.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: Both reviewers agree the empirical claims are supported by experiments on standard datasets and appropriate baselines, but the paper lacks formal proofs (e.g., for the stated O(nT) inference complexity and exactness of the generalized inside–outside), statistical significance testing, and released code for full reproducibility. Given the plausible but not formally justified algorithmic claims and the missing analysis of optimization/learning properties, a cautious mid-level score of 3 reflects reasonable empirical evidence tempered by important theoretical and reproducibility gaps.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper presents empirical evidence on standard datasets and compares to appropriate baselines, so the main empirical claim (better performance) is provisionally supported. However, from the summary there is no formal theorem or detailed worst-case analysis proving the claimed O(nT) inference complexity nor an argument proving exactness of the generalized inside–outside for the claimed class, which is critical given my emphasis on formal guarantees. The learning/optimization side lacks analysis of likelihood geometry or convergence (use of L-BFGS is stated but no discussion of convexity or surface), and no mention of statistical significance or released code limits reproducibility. These gaps justify a middling score: promising empirical work but missing the theoretical and optimization analyses I require for higher confidence.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper states clear claims and supports them with experiments on three standard datasets (ACE2004/2005, GENIA) against appropriate baselines (mention hypergraph, semi-CRF, constituency parser, etc.), and reports hyperparameters—so empirical support is convincing. The algorithmic description (multigraph log-linear model, O(nT) inside–outside inference and max-product decoding) matches standard structured-prediction tooling and is plausible; no obvious mathematical contradictions are indicated in the summary. However, the summary did not mention code release or statistical significance testing, and the paper admits a modeling limitation (inter-type interactions), so there are minor reproducibility and evaluation gaps that prevent a flawless score.

SUBSTANCE.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper provides solid empirical evaluation on standard overlapping-entity benchmarks with strong baselines and adequate reporting of hyperparameters, but both note the lack of code release and missing ablation/sensitivity analyses. Reviewer A further highlights important theoretical gaps (no formal proof of the claimed O(nT) inference complexity and no optimization/convergence analysis), which meaningfully weaken the paper’s claims. Given the strong experiments but notable theoretical and reproducibility shortcomings, a middle score of 3 is appropriate.

reviewer_a.justification: The experimental evaluation is adequate in that standard datasets (ACE2004/2005, GENIA) and reasonable baselines (mention hypergraph, CRF, semi-CRF, constituency parser, Illinois NER) are used and hyperparameters are reported, but there is no code release mentioned. From my theoretical-perspective the paper lacks crucial formal guarantees: the O(nT) inference claim is stated but I see no formal theorem or worst-case proof, and there is no analysis of the likelihood surface or convergence behaviour for L-BFGS. Ablations or sensitivity analyses appear missing (no investigation of which components drive the gains), though some limitations are acknowledged (e.g., not modeling certain cross-type interactions). These gaps make the work empirically reasonable but theoretically under-supported, so a middle score is appropriate.

reviewer_b.justification: The experimental evaluation is solid: three standard overlapping-entity benchmarks (ACE2004/2005, GENIA) and multiple strong baselines are used, with reported P/R/F and hyperparameters, supporting the main empirical claims. However, there is no mention of ablation or sensitivity analyses to isolate which modeling components drive gains, and code is not provided which hurts full reproducibility despite hyperparameter reporting. Related work appears adequately covered and the paper does acknowledge at least one limitation (missing some inter-type interactions). Overall the paper offers strong empirical evidence but misses a few practical analyses and release details I would expect for maximal reproducibility.

main_claims: Proposes a new multigraph-based structured prediction model capable of recognizing overlapping entities.
This is the first structured prediction model utilizing multigraphs to predict overlapping structures (as claimed).
The proposed model achieves better empirical performance than previous models (including the mention hypergraph and other baselines) on standard datasets.
Inference for the model has time complexity O(nT), matching the linear-time mention hypergraph model.
_summary.methodology: Define a multigraph-based log-linear model trained by regularized log-likelihood optimized with L-BFGS; perform inference with a generalized inside-outside dynamic programming (O(nT)) and decode with max-product MPE to extract entities.
limitations: The model does not model some interactions between distinct entity types (unlike the linear-chain CRF baseline).

reviewer_a.name: Theoretical Structured-Prediction Purist
reviewer_a.background: Senior researcher in structured prediction and theoretical machine learning, with a history of publishing formal analyses of inference algorithms and model identifiability.
traits: Downgrades papers that claim algorithmic complexity without a formal proof or detailed worst-case analysis (expects theorems/lemmas verifying O(·) bounds).
Penalizes empirical-heavy submissions that lack theoretical guarantees about expressivity/consistency or clear limits (expects formal characterization of representational power or counterexamples).
Demands detailed optimization/learning analysis: will deduct points if there is no discussion of likelihood surface, convexity/concavity, or convergence behavior of L-BFGS for the proposed model.
Requires explicit comparison to exact-inference baselines or proof that approximate/structured inference is exact for the claimed class; will lower score if this is missing.
reviewer_a.reviewing_style: Focuses on formal guarantees and theoretical novelty; skeptical of primarily empirical gains unless accompanied by rigorous proofs or theoretical insight.

reviewer_b.name: Applied NLP Pragmatist
reviewer_b.background: Experienced NLP researcher and practitioner specializing in named-entity recognition and sequence labeling, with many applied and benchmark-driven papers emphasizing practical gains and reproducibility.
traits: Rewards clear empirical improvements on standard benchmarks and will raise score for consistent SOTA or near-SOTA gains across multiple datasets (expects rigorous baselines and statistical significance).
Values practical algorithmic properties (e.g., linear-time inference) and will reward methods that match or improve runtime/space while improving accuracy, even without deep theoretical proofs.

Favors models that directly address known practical issues (here: overlapping entities) and will score higher if the method is simple to implement and likely useful in downstream systems (expects ablation and error analysis).
Gives credit for reproducibility: will increase recommendation if code, hyperparameters, and training details are provided to facilitate adoption.
reviewer_b.reviewing_style: Pragmatic and empirical: emphasizes utility, clear experimental validation, and reproducibility; tolerant of limited theory when practical impact is demonstrated.
